audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Leviathan speech: untamable sea-creature/chaos imagery, failed capture, terrifying presence, armor, flame/smoke poetry, weapon-resistance, and king over proud beasts. Preserved as mythic-poetic creature language.

verses.restored: Can you draw out Leviathan with a hook, or press down his tongue with a cord?
Can you put a rope in his nose, or pierce his jaw with a hook?
Will he make many pleas to you? Will he speak soft words to you?
Will he cut a covenant with you, that you take him as servant forever?
Will you play with him like a bird, or bind him for your young women?
Will partners bargain over him? Will they divide him among merchants?
Can you fill his skin with harpoons, or his head with fishing spears?
Lay your hand on him; remember the battle; you will not do it again.
Look, hope concerning him is false; is one not thrown down even at the sight of him?
No one is fierce enough to rouse him. Who then can stand before me?
Who has first given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under all the heavens is mine.
I will not keep silent about his limbs, his mighty strength, and his graceful frame.
Who can strip off his outer garment? Who can enter his double bridle?
Who can open the doors of his face? Around his teeth is terror.
His back is rows of shields, shut up with a tight seal.
One is so near another that no air can come between them.
They cling each to the other; they hold together and cannot be separated.
His sneezings flash light, and his eyes are like eyelids of dawn.
From his mouth go flaming torches; sparks of fire leap out.
From his nostrils smoke goes out, like a boiling pot and reeds.
His breath kindles coals, and flame goes out from his mouth.
In his neck strength lodges, and before him dismay dances.
The folds of his flesh cling together; they are firm upon him, not moved.
His heart is firm like stone, firm like the lower millstone.
When he rises, mighty ones are afraid; because of crashing they miss themselves.
The sword that reaches him does not stand, nor spear, dart, or javelin.
He counts iron as straw, bronze as rotten wood.
The arrow does not make him flee; sling stones are turned to stubble for him.
Clubs are counted as stubble; he laughs at the shaking of the javelin.
His undersides are sharp potsherds; he spreads a threshing sledge on the mud.
He makes the deep boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a jar of ointment.
Behind him he leaves a shining wake; one would think the deep had white hair.
On earth there is none like him, made without fear.
He sees every high thing; he is king over all the sons of pride.
